explanation: Presenting progress in monthly increments with formal validation and a comprehensive status review meets the customer's stated needs and ensures strong stakeholder engagement.

eco_task: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
topic: Engage Stakeholders and Build Shared Understanding
prompts: During planning, a project manager is struggling to identify stakeholders, and without input from the right stakeholders, gathering user feedback will be difficult. What is the best response?
In planning, stakeholder identification is stuck, making it hard to collect user feedback from the right people. What is the best response?
A project manager cannot readily identify stakeholders in planning, which will hinder gathering user feedback. What should the project manager do?
Without clear stakeholders in planning, user feedback will be hard to obtain. What is the best response for the project manager?

correct.text: Refer to the project charter to identify stakeholders
correct.why: The charter names purpose, objectives, and key stakeholders.

correct.text: Use the project charter as the source to identify stakeholders
correct.why: Key stakeholders are typically documented in the charter.

correct.text: Consult the project charter to find the initial stakeholders
correct.why: The charter is the starting point for stakeholder identity.

distractors.text: Review the stakeholder engagement plan
distractors.trap: That plan is created after stakeholders are identified.

distractors.text: Inform the executive sponsors
distractors.trap: Notifying sponsors is neither comprehensive nor efficient.

distractors.text: Review the project scope statement
distractors.trap: Scope is not the primary source for stakeholders.

distractors.text: Survey the entire organization for anyone interested
distractors.trap: Unfocused surveys are inefficient versus the charter.

distractors.text: Wait until execution to discover stakeholders organically
distractors.trap: Late discovery risks missing key input in planning.

distractors.text: Copy stakeholders from a similar past project's risk log
distractors.trap: Risk logs are not the right source for this project.

explanation: The project charter documents the project's purpose, objectives, and stakeholders, making it the most direct source for identifying the stakeholders critical to project success during planning.

prompts: A dispersed team has been working on a long-term development project. Just after an iteration finishes, a new team member is onboarded. During their first virtual retrospective, the new member interrupts and asks everyone to reiterate their roles and past work from previous iterations. What is the best response?

On a long-running distributed development project, a new member joins after an iteration ends. In the first virtual retrospective, they interrupt to request role recaps and prior-iteration history. What is the best response?

A new hire joins a dispersed long-term development team right after an iteration. At the virtual retrospective they interrupt, asking everyone to restate roles and past work. What should the project manager do?
After onboarding a new member to a dispersed long-term team, the first virtual retrospective is interrupted by a request to review roles and previous iteration work. What is the best response?

correct.text: Tell the team member that this question is best addressed in a separate meeting
correct.why: Keeps the retrospective focused while still honoring the need.

correct.text: Explain that roles and past work should be covered in a separate meeting
correct.why: Protects retro focus and still plans to meet the need.

correct.text: Defer the request to a dedicated onboarding session outside the retrospective
correct.why: Separate session answers onboarding without derailing the retro.

distractors.text: Direct the new member to review the project management plan after the meeting
distractors.trap: Deflects without addressing the onboarding need.

distractors.text: Ask each member to explain their role and past iteration work
distractors.trap: Derails the retrospective into an off-topic discussion.

distractors.text: Ask the new member's functional manager to review the onboarding process
distractors.trap: Shifts blame without answering the member's question.

distractors.text: Cancel the retrospective so the team can fully orient the new member
distractors.trap: Cancelling loses continuous improvement for the whole team.

distractors.text: Ignore the interruption and continue the retrospective without comment
distractors.trap: Ignoring the need damages inclusion of the new member.

distractors.text: Assign the new member offline reading and exclude them from the retro
distractors.trap: Exclusion blocks participation and does not teach roles.

explanation: A retrospective should stay focused on reviewing the iteration and identifying improvements; the project manager should politely defer the new member's onboarding questions to a separate, more suitable meeting.

prompts: During release planning, stakeholders disagree over how to prioritize their competing needs, and the ongoing dispute is causing the development team to lose focus. To align the stakeholders and resolve the conflict, which conflict resolution technique should the project manager use?
In release planning, stakeholders clash over competing priority needs, distracting the development team. Which conflict resolution technique should the project manager use to align them?
Stakeholders dispute prioritization of competing needs during release planning, and the development team is losing focus. Which technique should resolve the conflict and realign stakeholders?
Competing stakeholder needs are disputed in release planning, pulling the development team off track. Which conflict resolution technique should the project manager apply?

correct.text: Use collaborating to align stakeholder priorities through joint resolution.
correct.why: It incorporates viewpoints into a solution all parties can accept.

correct.text: Apply collaboration and problem-solving to resolve the priority dispute.
correct.why: It addresses root causes and builds a win-win agreement.

correct.text: Use collaborative problem solving to reach a shared priority decision.
correct.why: It works through differences to a mutually accepted priority.

distractors.text: Use forcing to impose one stakeholder's priority decision.
distractors.trap: It leaves others' concerns unaddressed.

distractors.text: Use withdrawing to step away from the priority disagreement.
distractors.trap: It lets the team distraction continue.

distractors.text: Use smoothing to emphasize agreement and minimize differences.
distractors.trap: It downplays differences without solving the dispute.

distractors.text: Use compromising so each stakeholder gives up some priority needs.
distractors.trap: It splits differences without fully resolving priorities.

distractors.text: Use avoiding to postpone the stakeholder prioritization conflict.
distractors.trap: It leaves stakeholder misalignment in place.

distractors.text: Use directing to tell stakeholders which priorities will win.
distractors.trap: Top-down orders fail to align needs.

explanation: Collaborating brings the differing perspectives together to find a mutually acceptable solution, resolving the root cause. Forcing, withdrawing, and smoothing all fail to genuinely address the stakeholders' concerns.

prompts: A project is delayed because the functional manager decided to reassign resources to provide onsite support for another project, without consulting the parties affected. Which action should the project manager take first?
Without consulting those affected, a functional manager reassigns resources to onsite support on another project, delaying this work. What should the project manager do first?
A functional manager moves people to support another project onsite and does not consult affected parties, causing delay. Which action comes first for the project manager?
Resources were reassigned by a functional manager to another project's onsite support without involving affected parties, delaying the project. What is the project manager's first action?

correct.text: Assess the impact of the delay on schedule and cost.
correct.why: Impact first enables informed communication and recovery planning.

correct.text: Evaluate how the delay affects schedule and cost.
correct.why: Knowing schedule and cost impact guides next steps.

correct.text: Determine the schedule and cost impact caused by the delay.
correct.why: Quantified impact supports effective recovery and messaging.

distractors.text: Share the work schedule with the functional manager.
distractors.trap: Communicates before knowing the actual disruption impact.

distractors.text: Meet with stakeholders to agree on a communications approach.
distractors.trap: Focuses on process before impact is quantified.

distractors.text: Inform the customer of the delay and set a new delivery date.
distractors.trap: Commits to a new date before impact is assessed.

distractors.text: File a formal complaint against the functional manager immediately.
distractors.trap: Blame first does not quantify or fix the delay.

distractors.text: Request the sponsor replace the reassigned resources today.
distractors.trap: Resource asks come after impact is understood.

distractors.text: Update the risk register only and wait for the next review.
distractors.trap: Passive logging delays needed impact assessment.

explanation: The project manager must first assess how the delay affects schedule and cost. Only then can they communicate accurately with stakeholders and develop a plan to reduce the delay.

prompts: During execution of a clinical systems modernization effort, the customer's project manager resigns and is not replaced. The internal project manager discovers that, as a result, key stakeholders are no longer receiving updated information on time. What should the project manager do?

On a clinical systems modernization project in execution, the customer project manager resigns with no replacement. Key stakeholders then stop getting timely updates. What should the internal project manager do?

Mid-execution on a clinical systems modernization, the customer loses their project manager and does not backfill the role. Key stakeholders miss timely information. What should the project manager do?
The customer's project manager on a clinical systems modernization resigns without a successor, and key stakeholders no longer get updates on time. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Revise the stakeholder communications approach.
correct.why: Stakeholder changes require updating communications to restore flow.

correct.text: Update the communications approach for the affected stakeholders.
correct.why: A changed stakeholder landscape needs a revised communication plan.

correct.text: Adjust the stakeholder communications approach to restore timely updates.
correct.why: Revising the approach fixes the broken information path.

distractors.text: Immediately inform the key stakeholders directly.
distractors.trap: Patches one gap without fixing the disrupted process.

distractors.text: Inform the executive sponsors of the recent changes.
distractors.trap: Raises awareness but does not fix communication flow.

distractors.text: Keep sending the information until a reply is received.
distractors.trap: Repeats a broken method without fixing the channel.

distractors.text: Pause all external reporting until a replacement is hired.
distractors.trap: Pausing leaves stakeholders uninformed even longer.

distractors.text: Escalate to legal for breach of the customer agreement.
distractors.trap: Legal escalation is premature for a communication gap.

distractors.text: Assign a junior team member as the permanent customer project manager.
distractors.trap: Staffing the customer role is not the PM's authority.

explanation: When a key stakeholder role changes, the communications approach must be updated to restore reliable, timely information flow. Notifying individuals or sponsors, or resending updates, does not repair the underlying process.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A portfolio support office is adopting more adaptive practices and has hired a new project manager with hybrid experience. The team is slow to adopt the new methodology and is not updating project artifacts in a timely manner. What should the project manager do to improve the team's performance?

A program support group is moving toward adaptive delivery, and a recently hired project manager has experience with hybrid work. Delivery specialists are not updating artifacts on time and seem slow to use the new approach. What should the project manager do to improve performance?

In a financial services PMO, a team is shifting from a mostly predictive process to more adaptive practices. Although the new project manager has hybrid experience, artifacts are late and the team has not embraced the method. What should the project manager do?

A support office in a public agency is introducing a hybrid delivery method. Team members continue using old habits and delay updating project records. What is the best action for the project manager to improve team performance?

correct.text: Set up training for all project delivery specialists.
correct.why: Shared training addresses the adoption gap causing inconsistent use of the new method.

correct.text: Arrange a team training session on the new methodology.
correct.why: The issue is a common skills gap, so group training targets the root cause.

correct.text: Provide methodology training to the full delivery team.
correct.why: Building common understanding helps the team apply the approach and update artifacts consistently.

distractors.text: Email the team and require all artifacts be updated by today.
distractors.trap: Demands compliance without building needed capability.

distractors.text: Ask functional managers to correct the delivery specialists' behavior.
distractors.trap: Escalates before addressing the team knowledge gap.

distractors.text: Meet separately with each member to request faster updates.
distractors.trap: Uses reminders instead of fixing shared confusion.

distractors.text: Replace the least responsive specialists with adaptive practitioners.
distractors.trap: Jumps to staffing changes before coaching.

distractors.text: Add artifact completion dates to the risk register.
distractors.trap: Tracks a symptom without improving adoption.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to mandate use of the new methodology.
distractors.trap: A mandate does not create competence.

explanation: Any team needs time and support to adopt a new methodology, so a training session builds the shared understanding that resolves the slow adoption. Reminders, escalation, or one-on-ones do not close the underlying knowledge gap.

prompts: A virtual team is midway through a sprint on a complex task and is ahead of schedule. During the daily sync, a new team member interrupts and asks everyone to describe what they worked on before the current sprint. As project manager, how should you handle this?

A distributed agile team is in the middle of a sprint and progressing faster than planned. In the daily standup, a recently added developer asks each person to explain their earlier work before this sprint. How should the project manager respond?

During a daily sync for a remote team, a new analyst interrupts the flow and asks for a history of everyone's prior assignments. The sprint is active and the team is ahead of schedule. What should the project manager do?

A new member joins a virtual product team during an ongoing sprint. At the daily meeting, they ask the group to walk through work completed before the current sprint. What is the best way for the project manager to handle it?

correct.text: Explain that the daily sync is not the right time for that discussion.
correct.why: It protects the meeting purpose while responding respectfully to the newcomer.

correct.text: Politely defer the question because it is outside the daily sync's purpose.
correct.why: Daily syncs should stay focused on current progress, plans, and impediments.

correct.text: Tell the new member this topic should be handled outside the daily sync.
correct.why: The response keeps the standup productive without dismissing the question.

distractors.text: Direct the new member to the project documentation after the meeting.
distractors.trap: Provides little orientation or immediate meeting control.

distractors.text: Ask the new member to leave for interrupting the meeting.
distractors.trap: Punitive and disproportionate for a valid question.

distractors.text: Have every specialist summarize their completed pre-sprint work.
distractors.trap: Turns the sync into an off-topic history review.

distractors.text: Extend the daily sync so the new member can catch up.
distractors.trap: Sacrifices the timebox and meeting focus.

distractors.text: Cancel the sync and schedule a full project walkthrough.
distractors.trap: Overreacts and disrupts current sprint communication.

distractors.text: Ask the scrum master to document the interruption as an impediment.
distractors.trap: Misclassifies a facilitation issue as an impediment.

explanation: The daily sync is time-boxed for current work, so the project manager politely explains it is not the right forum and can schedule a separate time to onboard the new member. Redirecting to docs, expelling them, or indulging the request all harm the meeting or morale.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A project manager is trying to compare the performance of two agile teams. For the same scope of work, team A estimated and delivered 100 story points and team B estimated and delivered 125 story points. Which team is performing better?

Two agile teams completed the same scope. One team estimated and delivered 100 story points, while the other estimated and delivered 125 story points. How should the project manager determine which team performed better?

A product organization wants to compare two Scrum teams that delivered the same scope. Team A's work totaled 100 story points, and team B's work totaled 125 story points. Which team should be considered the better performer?

A project manager reviews two agile teams after equivalent work is completed. Their story point totals differ, with team A at 100 and team B at 125. What is the best basis for judging better performance?

correct.text: The team with the fewest defects in its deliverables.
correct.why: Story points estimate size; quality shows whether the output is actually performing well.

correct.text: The team that delivered the same scope with fewer defects.
correct.why: Quality is a better performance indicator than comparing point totals across teams.

correct.text: The team whose completed work has the lowest defect count.
correct.why: Point values are team-relative, while defect levels reflect delivered quality.

distractors.text: Team A, because it delivered fewer story points.
distractors.trap: Lower points do not mean better performance.

distractors.text: Team B, because it delivered more story points.
distractors.trap: Higher points do not prove greater performance.

distractors.text: The team that closed the most backlog items.
distractors.trap: Counts items without considering quality.

distractors.text: The team with the most accurate original estimate.
distractors.trap: Estimate accuracy is not the main comparison here.

distractors.text: The team with the highest velocity trend.
distractors.trap: Velocity is team-specific and not directly comparable.

distractors.text: The team that finished its sprint ceremonies fastest.
distractors.trap: Meeting speed does not measure delivery performance.

explanation: Story points are a relative measure of size and complexity, not of quality or performance, and teams estimate them differently, so counts are not comparable. Agile values delivering working software with quality, making the fewest-defects team the better performer.

prompts: A dispersed project team has just finished a complex sprint ahead of schedule. During the retrospective, a new team member interrupts and asks everyone to explain their roles and what they plan to work on in the next sprint. What should the project manager do?

A distributed team completes a difficult sprint early. In the retrospective, a newly assigned member asks the group to describe each person's role and upcoming sprint work. What should the project manager do?

During a retrospective for a remote product team, a new tester asks everyone to explain their responsibilities and next sprint plans. The team has just completed a complex sprint ahead of schedule. How should the project manager respond?

An agile team is holding its retrospective after finishing a complex sprint early. A new colleague interrupts to ask for role introductions and future work assignments. What is the best project manager response?

correct.text: Tell the team this topic should be handled in a separate meeting.
correct.why: It preserves the retrospective while creating an appropriate space for orientation.

correct.text: Defer the role and next-sprint discussion to another session.
correct.why: The retrospective should focus on improvement, not onboarding or sprint planning.

correct.text: Explain that a separate meeting is better for that discussion.
correct.why: It keeps the retrospective focused and still supports the new member.

distractors.text: Answer the new member's questions immediately in the retrospective.
distractors.trap: Diverts the meeting from reflection and improvement.

distractors.text: Ask each person to summarize their role and next assignment.
distractors.trap: Converts the retrospective into onboarding and planning.

distractors.text: Schedule individual introductions with every team member.
distractors.trap: Inefficient for information the group can cover together.

distractors.text: Add role clarification to the retrospective action items.
distractors.trap: Treats onboarding as a process improvement action.

distractors.text: Ask the product owner to explain the next sprint backlog.
distractors.trap: Moves into sprint planning instead of retrospective learning.

distractors.text: Tell the new member to wait until they understand agile ceremonies.
distractors.trap: Dismissive and fails to support onboarding.

explanation: A retrospective is focused on reviewing work and improving processes, so the project manager politely defers the newcomer's request to a more appropriate time. Answering immediately, polling the team, or arranging one-on-ones would derail or inefficiently handle the situation.

prompts: A company launches a new strategic project whose goal and scope were only loosely defined at an executive committee meeting, and stakeholders have been appointed. The project manager needs to hold the kickoff meeting, but most stakeholders are in different locations and some are on vacation. What should the project manager do?

An enterprise initiative has been approved with broad goals and initial stakeholders named. The project manager must conduct the kickoff, but the stakeholder group is geographically dispersed and several are away. What is the best action?

A healthcare organization starts a strategic project after executives define the scope only at a high level. Stakeholders are assigned, but they work in different locations and some are unavailable. How should the project manager conduct the kickoff?
A new strategic project needs a kickoff after an executive committee gave only loose direction. Because many stakeholders are remote and some are on leave, what should the project manager do?

correct.text: Arrange a virtual kickoff meeting with the stakeholders.
correct.why: A virtual kickoff supports broad participation despite location and availability constraints.

correct.text: Hold the kickoff virtually with the stakeholder group.
correct.why: It enables shared alignment without waiting for everyone to be physically present.

correct.text: Schedule an online kickoff for all appointed stakeholders.
correct.why: Remote access allows dispersed stakeholders to engage and absent members to catch up.

distractors.text: Hold the kickoff only with available stakeholders and brief others later.
distractors.trap: Creates inconsistent alignment across the stakeholder group.

distractors.text: Delay the kickoff until all stakeholders return to the office.
distractors.trap: Unnecessarily postpones the project start.

distractors.text: Meet one-on-one with each stakeholder before kickoff.
distractors.trap: Misses the shared alignment of a group kickoff.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to conduct the kickoff instead.
distractors.trap: Delegates a project manager responsibility without need.

distractors.text: Send the project charter and request email acknowledgments.
distractors.trap: One-way communication is not a kickoff.

distractors.text: Start execution and schedule the kickoff after vacations end.
distractors.trap: Begins work before stakeholder alignment.

explanation: A virtual kickoff engages all stakeholders regardless of location or availability and can be recorded for anyone who cannot attend live. Meeting with only some, delaying, or holding individual sessions all sacrifice alignment or timing.

prompts: An experienced project manager is leading a large agile project. The ground rules were communicated to the team at the start, but after 12 iterations the project manager notices that several delivery team members are no longer following them. What should the project manager do first to restore adherence?

A large agile team agreed to ground rules early in the project. After 12 iterations, the project manager sees that some delivery team members have drifted away from those agreements. What should the project manager do first?

On a major agile initiative, the project manager introduced working agreements at the beginning. Several iterations later, multiple team members are no longer observing them. What is the first appropriate action?
A seasoned project manager notices that team ground rules set at project start are being ignored by several people after many iterations. How should the project manager first respond?

correct.text: Hold a team meeting to reinforce the ground rules.
correct.why: The project manager should first coach the team and renew shared agreements.

correct.text: Meet with the project team to revisit the ground rules.
correct.why: Reinforcing team agreements is appropriate before escalation or discipline.

correct.text: Facilitate a discussion reminding the team of the ground rules.
correct.why: Servant leadership starts with facilitation and shared accountability.

distractors.text: Ask human resources to warn the noncompliant members.
distractors.trap: Escalates to discipline too early.

distractors.text: Ask functional managers to replace members who ignore the rules.
distractors.trap: Removes people before coaching the team.

distractors.text: Tell the sponsor to initiate disciplinary action.
distractors.trap: Escalates before direct team engagement.

distractors.text: Update the issue log and wait for the retrospective.
distractors.trap: Documents the concern without addressing behavior.

distractors.text: Privately criticize each member who violated the rules.
distractors.trap: Frames shared agreements as individual punishment.

distractors.text: Rewrite the ground rules without team discussion.
distractors.trap: Undermines team ownership of agreements.

explanation: An agile project manager acts as a servant leader, so the first step is to re-engage the team and reinforce the agreed ground rules before escalating to HR, functional managers, or the sponsor.

prompts: A newly formed team has adopted agile practices and is meeting expectations, but the project lead notices that many delivery team members have grown bored with the daily routine. What should the project lead do?
A recently assembled agile team is delivering as expected, yet several members appear disengaged by the repetitive routine. What should the project lead do?
A product team new to agile is meeting its commitments, but the project lead senses that many specialists are becoming bored with how work is done each day. What is the best response?
An agile team formed earlier in the project is performing adequately. The project lead notices low energy and boredom despite acceptable results. How should the lead respond?

correct.text: Challenge the team to find ways to reach higher performance.
correct.why: It energizes the team through continuous improvement and shared ownership.

correct.text: Encourage the team to improve how they work and perform.
correct.why: Agile teams should inspect, adapt, and pursue better performance together.

correct.text: Invite the team to identify new ways to improve performance.
correct.why: The lead should stimulate team-level growth rather than accept routine stagnation.

distractors.text: Wait for the team to self-correct during a future retrospective.
distractors.trap: A new team still benefits from active leadership.

distractors.text: Ask each member privately what routine they dislike.
distractors.trap: Focuses on individuals instead of team improvement.

distractors.text: Report the boredom to senior management for direction.
distractors.trap: Escalates a team leadership issue unnecessarily.

distractors.text: Add more mandatory status meetings to increase engagement.
distractors.trap: More routine meetings can worsen disengagement.

distractors.text: Rotate team members off the project to prevent boredom.
distractors.trap: Disrupts a team that is meeting expectations.

distractors.text: Ignore the boredom because commitments are still being met.
distractors.trap: Misses an opportunity for continuous improvement.

explanation: Agile encourages teams to continually seek better ways of working and higher performance, so the project lead should challenge this newly formed team to raise its own bar rather than escalate or defer.

prompts: A project manager discovers that a stakeholder is giving inaccurate status reports to the oversight board and does not actually have correct information about the project. Which action should the project manager take first?

A stakeholder has been briefing the governance board with incorrect project status information. The project manager learns the stakeholder does not have accurate data. What should the project manager do first?
During execution, a project manager finds that an influential stakeholder is reporting inaccurate progress to the steering committee because they lack current information. What is the first response?

A project manager hears that a department representative has sent incorrect status updates to the oversight group. The representative appears misinformed rather than malicious. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Meet with the stakeholder to discuss the inaccurate reporting and share accurate information.
correct.why: Direct conversation corrects the source and clarifies why the reporting is wrong.

correct.text: Talk with the stakeholder directly and provide the correct project information.
correct.why: Addressing the person first resolves misinformation before broader communication changes.

correct.text: Meet the stakeholder to clarify the facts and discuss the reports.
correct.why: It surfaces the cause and corrects the information without bypassing them.

distractors.text: Revise the communications plan so the stakeholder receives updates.
distractors.trap: Useful later, but not the first correction.

distractors.text: Send future status updates directly to the oversight board.
distractors.trap: Bypasses the stakeholder instead of fixing misinformation.

distractors.text: Require all status reports be approved before release.
distractors.trap: Adds excessive control before understanding the issue.

distractors.text: Ask the board to ignore the stakeholder's future reports.
distractors.trap: Damages relationships and avoids root cause.

distractors.text: Remove the stakeholder from the communications distribution list.
distractors.trap: Would make their information gap worse.

distractors.text: Escalate the stakeholder's behavior to the executive sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalates before a direct conversation.

explanation: Meeting with the stakeholder lets the project manager identify why the reporting is inaccurate and correct it at the source, which the other options fail to do.

prompts: An organizational transformation project has two workstreams running in parallel between corporate headquarters and an overseas office. Before kickoff, the project manager is scheduled to meet the overseas office leader. What should the meeting focus on?

A transformation effort will run parallel workstreams at headquarters and at an international branch. Before the kickoff, the project manager will meet with the branch leader. What should be the focus?

A company is preparing a change initiative involving both the central office and a regional office abroad. The project manager has a pre-kickoff meeting with the regional leader. What should they discuss?
Before launching a transformation project split between headquarters and an overseas site, the project manager meets the site leader. What is the most appropriate focus for that meeting?

correct.text: Explain the project's strategic value, key deliverables, and expected involvement.
correct.why: Pre-kickoff engagement should align expectations and clarify the stakeholder's role.

correct.text: Discuss the project's value, main deliverables, and the leader's participation.
correct.why: Early stakeholder alignment helps ensure shared understanding before kickoff.

correct.text: Set expectations about project purpose, deliverables, and the office's involvement.
correct.why: The meeting should establish alignment before detailed execution decisions.

distractors.text: Agree on procurement strategies for the overseas workstream.
distractors.trap: Too detailed for a pre-kickoff alignment meeting.

distractors.text: Prepare the leader to brief headquarters staff about the project.
distractors.trap: Premature before expectations are aligned.

distractors.text: Assign a subject matter expert to represent the overseas team.
distractors.trap: Staffing details belong after initial alignment.

distractors.text: Negotiate the overseas office's final project budget.
distractors.trap: Focuses on execution details, not kickoff readiness.

distractors.text: Review individual task assignments for both workstreams.
distractors.trap: Task planning is premature before kickoff.

distractors.text: Audit the overseas office's previous transformation results.
distractors.trap: Does not set expectations for this project.

explanation: Because the meeting precedes kickoff, its purpose is to set expectations by conveying the project's strategic value, key deliverables, and the leader's expected involvement so both parties are aligned; the other tasks come later.

prompts: You are working with a Scrum team that keeps missing deadlines, and the oversight board is worried the project may not deliver its expected value. Your analysis reveals a competency mismatch within one of the teams. What is the best response?

A Scrum team has repeatedly missed delivery dates, and the governance board is concerned about value realization. After reviewing the situation, you find a skills mismatch in one team. What should you do?

On an agile project, one team is falling behind and leadership questions whether the expected benefits will be achieved. Your assessment points to a competency gap among team members. What is the best response?
A product delivery team is missing commitments. Analysis shows the root issue is not motivation or scope, but a mismatch between required and available skills. How should the project manager respond?

correct.text: Provide appropriate training to close the competency gap.
correct.why: Training directly addresses the skills mismatch causing missed deadlines.

correct.text: Offer targeted training for the team with the skills mismatch.
correct.why: Building capability is the right response to a competency shortfall.

correct.text: Arrange training to refresh and strengthen the needed competencies.
correct.why: The root cause is capability, so skill development is needed.

distractors.text: Accept the risk that deadlines may continue slipping.
distractors.trap: Passive when the root cause is known.

distractors.text: Revise the schedule to reflect the team's slower delivery.
distractors.trap: Adjusts dates without fixing the gap.

distractors.text: Remind the team that deadlines are important.
distractors.trap: Motivation cannot replace missing competence.

distractors.text: Escalate the team to the oversight board for correction.
distractors.trap: Escalates instead of developing capability.

distractors.text: Reduce scope until the current skills are sufficient.
distractors.trap: Avoids the skill issue and may reduce value.

distractors.text: Replace the entire team with more experienced members.
distractors.trap: Disruptive before trying skill development.

explanation: The root cause is a skills gap, so providing appropriate training addresses it directly; accepting the risk, rescheduling, or urging the team does not fix the mismatch.

prompts: A client has contracted a company to manage a large agile project with many subcontractors. The client is issuing orders directly to the subcontractors instead of to the contractor's project manager, creating conflict within the team. What should the project manager do to prevent this from happening again?

On a large agile engagement, the customer bypasses the contractor's project manager and gives instructions directly to subcontractors. This is creating team conflict. What should the project manager do to prevent recurrence?

A vendor is managing a complex project involving several subcontractors. The client has begun directing those subcontractors directly, which has caused confusion and conflict. What is the best preventive action?
During an agile project with multiple subcontractors, a client repeatedly gives work orders to subcontractor staff instead of through the project manager. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Ask the client not to give direct orders to the subcontractors.
correct.why: The behavior's source must be addressed directly to prevent further conflict.

correct.text: Speak with the client and request that instructions go through the project manager.
correct.why: Clarifying the communication boundary stops the bypass that is causing conflict.

correct.text: Tell the client to route subcontractor direction through the project manager.
correct.why: Directly setting the boundary prevents repeated conflicting instructions.

distractors.text: Keep the team informed about the client's expectations.
distractors.trap: Awareness does not stop direct orders.

distractors.text: Invite the client to the team planning meeting.
distractors.trap: More access may not fix the bypass.

distractors.text: Tell the team to avoid conflict with the client.
distractors.trap: Ignores the client's role in causing conflict.

distractors.text: Ask subcontractors to ignore all client communication.
distractors.trap: Overcorrects and harms stakeholder collaboration.

distractors.text: Escalate immediately to legal for contract enforcement.
distractors.trap: Premature before direct stakeholder discussion.

distractors.text: Reassign the subcontractors to separate work packages.
distractors.trap: Changes structure without addressing the client behavior.

explanation: The conflict stems from the client bypassing the project manager, so directly asking the client to route instructions through the PM addresses the root cause more effectively than the other options.

prompts: You are managing an infrastructure upgrade that is well into the execution phase. The sponsor asks you to confirm that all key stakeholders remain committed to the project. How should you assess the stakeholders' current engagement levels?

During execution of a facilities modernization project, the sponsor wants to know whether important stakeholders are still engaged and supportive. Which tool should the project manager use to assess current engagement?
A project sponsor asks for evidence that key stakeholders remain committed midway through an infrastructure project. How should the project manager assess their engagement levels?
An execution-phase upgrade project depends on several influential stakeholders. The sponsor asks you to evaluate how engaged they are now. What should you review?

correct.text: Review the stakeholder engagement assessment matrix.
correct.why: It compares current and desired engagement for each stakeholder.

correct.text: Use the stakeholder engagement assessment matrix.
correct.why: The matrix is designed to assess stakeholder engagement levels.

correct.text: Assess them through the stakeholder engagement assessment matrix.
correct.why: It shows whether stakeholders are unaware, resistant, neutral, supportive, or leading.

distractors.text: Review and update the stakeholder communications approach.
distractors.trap: Communication flow is not engagement level.

distractors.text: Analyze the most recent issue log entries.
distractors.trap: Issues do not measure stakeholder commitment.

distractors.text: Ask stakeholders to sign a commitment declaration.
distractors.trap: A formality is not an engagement assessment.

distractors.text: Review the resource management plan for stakeholder assignments.
distractors.trap: Resources are not stakeholder engagement states.

distractors.text: Check the risk register for stakeholder-related risks.
distractors.trap: Risks may inform concerns, not current engagement.

distractors.text: Measure attendance at the last project status meeting.
distractors.trap: Attendance alone does not show commitment.

explanation: The stakeholder engagement assessment matrix compares each stakeholder's current engagement against the desired level, making it the right tool to gauge current commitment.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A large corporation is transitioning from a predictive to an agile approach. A team well-versed in agile practices is in significant conflict with executives over the processes to follow. How should these conflicts be resolved?
An enterprise is moving from waterfall-style delivery to agile ways of working. Agile team members are clashing with senior executives about which processes should be used. What should be done?

During an agile transformation at a large organization, experienced agile practitioners disagree strongly with executives about process expectations. How should the project manager help resolve the conflict?
A company adopting agile has a delivery team that understands agile well, but executives continue challenging the process choices. What is the best way to address the conflict?

correct.text: Train the executives on agile practices.
correct.why: Executives need agile understanding to align with the transformation.

correct.text: Provide agile training for the executives.
correct.why: Leadership must understand agile principles before process conflicts can be resolved.

correct.text: Educate the executives on agile ways of working.
correct.why: Building executive mindset alignment reduces conflict over process expectations.

distractors.text: Negotiate a process compromise with the executives.
distractors.trap: Compromise before understanding may undermine agile adoption.

distractors.text: Invite executives to observe every team retrospective.
distractors.trap: Observation alone does not build agile mindset.

distractors.text: Require executives to follow agile practices immediately.
distractors.trap: Mandates can increase resistance without understanding.

distractors.text: Ask the agile team to use predictive processes temporarily.
distractors.trap: Avoids the transformation goal and root conflict.

distractors.text: Escalate the conflict to the transformation sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalation does not address executive knowledge gaps.

distractors.text: Document the disagreement in the issue log only.
distractors.trap: Recording conflict is not resolving it.

explanation: In a digital transformation, executives as key stakeholders must also acquire the agile mindset, so training them is the root-cause resolution that must precede negotiation, inclusion, or mandates.

prompts: During execution, the executive sponsor decides to replace a subject matter expert (SME) on the project. What is the best response?
A sponsor informs the project manager during execution that the current SME will be replaced. What should the project manager do?
Midway through delivery, leadership assigns a new subject matter expert to replace the current one. What is the project manager's best response?
A critical SME is being removed from an active project and a replacement is expected to join. How should the project manager handle the transition?

correct.text: Ensure sufficient knowledge transfer between the SME and the replacement.
correct.why: The successor needs project context and tacit knowledge to continue effectively.

correct.text: Arrange knowledge transfer from the current SME to the new SME.
correct.why: A planned handover reduces disruption from the personnel change.

correct.text: Facilitate a handover so the replacement receives needed SME knowledge.
correct.why: Managing the transition preserves continuity during execution.

distractors.text: Negotiate with the sponsor to keep the current SME.
distractors.trap: Resists the decision instead of managing the transition.

distractors.text: Tell the replacement to rely on the knowledge base.
distractors.trap: Repositories may miss tacit project knowledge.

distractors.text: Meet with the sponsor to discuss team conflicts.
distractors.trap: Introduces an unrelated issue.

distractors.text: Pause execution until the new SME is fully productive.
distractors.trap: Unnecessarily delays work without a transition plan.

distractors.text: Reassign all SME responsibilities to the project manager.
distractors.trap: The PM should manage, not replace, expertise.

distractors.text: Update the stakeholder register and take no further action.
distractors.trap: Administrative updates do not transfer knowledge.

explanation: When an SME is replaced, the project manager should ensure adequate knowledge transfer to the successor, since a knowledge base alone may not capture all the SME's project knowledge.

prompts: Shortly after a sprint begins, you receive an email from a key stakeholder who is unfamiliar with agile methods. They have a serious complaint about the newly started work and insist that one deliverable be changed. What is the best response?
Soon after sprint work starts, a key business stakeholder who does not understand agile sends a serious complaint and asks for a deliverable change. What should the project manager do?
A sprint has just begun when an important stakeholder objects to the work and demands a change to one deliverable. The stakeholder is new to agile practices. What is the best response?
Early in a sprint, a stakeholder unfamiliar with agile raises a major concern and wants the team to change a deliverable. How should the project manager handle the request?

correct.text: Schedule a meeting with the stakeholder and product owner to discuss the request.
correct.why: The product owner manages backlog decisions and should be involved in the discussion.

correct.text: Meet with the stakeholder and product owner to review the requested change.
correct.why: Change requests affecting product work should involve the product owner.

correct.text: Bring the stakeholder and product owner together to discuss the concern.
correct.why: It respects stakeholder input while preserving product owner backlog authority.

distractors.text: Raise the request in the daily scrum for the team's opinion.
distractors.trap: The daily scrum is not for change decisions.

distractors.text: Change the backlog and direct the team to start immediately.
distractors.trap: Bypasses the product owner's backlog authority.

distractors.text: Cancel the sprint and hold a priority retrospective.
distractors.trap: A drastic response for an initial request.

distractors.text: Reject the request because the sprint has already started.
distractors.trap: Dismisses stakeholder input without review.

distractors.text: Ask the scrum team to vote on whether to change scope.
distractors.trap: The team does not own product prioritization.

distractors.text: Forward the complaint to the sponsor for a decision.
distractors.trap: Escalates before using the product owner role.

explanation: Because the product owner owns the backlog, the project manager should bring the stakeholder and product owner together to discuss the request rather than acting through the daily scrum, unilaterally changing the backlog, or canceling the sprint.

prompts: A company is implementing a new computerized system. During execution, the head of a department critical to completion raises that the department's needs are entirely absent from the approved project management plan. What should the project manager have done to avoid this?

During execution of a new information system, a critical department leader says their department's requirements are missing from the approved plan. What should the project manager have done earlier to prevent this?
A digital implementation is underway when a key department head discovers the project plan does not include their operational needs. What planning action would have avoided this problem?

A major system rollout is in execution. A department essential to project success was not represented in the approved plan, and its leader raises the omission. What should have been done during planning?

correct.text: Involved all relevant stakeholders in project planning.
correct.why: Including the department head would have helped capture those needs.

correct.text: Engaged all relevant stakeholders while developing the project plan.
correct.why: Stakeholder involvement during planning prevents critical requirements from being missed.

correct.text: Included the critical department's stakeholders in planning activities.
correct.why: Their participation was needed to identify and incorporate department needs.

distractors.text: Managed the expectations of all relevant stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Expectations management cannot capture omitted needs.

distractors.text: Sought expert judgment when designing project scope.
distractors.trap: Experts do not replace stakeholder engagement.

distractors.text: Developed a change management plan to authorize changes.
distractors.trap: Change control does not prevent missed planning input.

distractors.text: Asked the sponsor to approve all department requirements.
distractors.trap: Approval does not ensure stakeholders were consulted.

distractors.text: Created a detailed issue escalation path during planning.
distractors.trap: Escalation handles issues after they appear.

distractors.text: Published the approved plan more widely after signoff.
distractors.trap: Late distribution would not gather missing needs.

explanation: The department head is a relevant stakeholder whose needs are missing, which indicates the project manager failed to involve all relevant stakeholders during planning; the other options would not have surfaced the omitted needs.

prompts: A team member is hosting a personal event and invites several colleagues through their work calendars. Another team member, who was not invited, becomes noticeably distracted during several standup meetings. What is the best response?

One project team member sends calendar invitations for a personal gathering to several coworkers. A colleague who did not receive an invitation seems distracted during multiple standups. What should the project manager do?

A delivery specialist uses work calendars to invite some teammates to a private event. Another teammate, not included in the invite, appears unfocused in recent daily meetings. What is the best response?
After a social invitation goes to some but not all team members, one unincluded member becomes visibly distracted in standups. How should the project manager respond?

correct.text: Set up a meeting with the distracted team member to discuss any issues.
correct.why: A private conversation helps uncover the cause without embarrassing the person.

correct.text: Meet privately with the distracted member to understand their concern.
correct.why: The project manager should first learn what is affecting the member's focus.

correct.text: Have a one-on-one discussion with the distracted team member.
correct.why: Direct, private dialogue is appropriate before acting on assumptions.

distractors.text: Ask the host to mark the calendar invitation as private.
distractors.trap: Treats the symptom before understanding the issue.

distractors.text: Tell the host to use private channels for personal invitations.
distractors.trap: Assumes the invitation method caused the distraction.

distractors.text: Add social event rules to the project charter.
distractors.trap: Social invitations do not belong in the charter.

distractors.text: Ask the distracted member publicly why they are unfocused.
distractors.trap: Embarrasses the member and may worsen trust.

distractors.text: Require all personal events to include the full team.
distractors.trap: Overreaches into personal relationships.

distractors.text: Ignore the distraction unless performance metrics decline.
distractors.trap: Avoids an early people issue.

explanation: Because the distraction may signal a broader concern, the project manager should first meet privately with the affected member to understand the cause, rather than adjusting invitations or the charter.

prompts: A serious quality issue has surfaced during a major public infrastructure project and is likely to cause a substantial delay to the committed completion date. As the project manager, what should you do?
On a large civic works project, a significant quality problem emerges and may delay the promised finish date. What should the project manager do?
A major transportation project encounters a serious quality concern that could affect the committed completion date. How should the project manager respond?
During a public-sector infrastructure initiative, the team identifies a major quality issue with likely schedule impact. What is the best project manager action?

correct.text: Meet with the project team and stakeholders to discuss the issue and possible solutions.
correct.why: Transparent collaboration aligns stakeholders and supports an agreed response.

correct.text: Bring the team and stakeholders together to review the issue and options.
correct.why: A major quality delay needs shared understanding and collaborative problem solving.

correct.text: Discuss the quality issue and solution options with the team and stakeholders.
correct.why: Open communication is required when quality problems may affect commitments.

distractors.text: Ignore the issue and hope it does not delay the project.
distractors.trap: Avoidance lets the problem worsen.

distractors.text: Delay the project until the quality issue is fully resolved.
distractors.trap: Acts unilaterally without stakeholder input.

distractors.text: Implement a fix without notifying the stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Lacks transparency for a major issue.

distractors.text: Ask the team to work overtime until the delay is recovered.
distractors.trap: Addresses schedule pressure without solving quality collaboratively.

distractors.text: Report the delay only after the new completion date is known.
distractors.trap: Withholds timely information from stakeholders.

distractors.text: Transfer the quality issue to the vendor and continue execution.
distractors.trap: Avoids owning stakeholder communication and response.

explanation: Meeting with the team and stakeholders brings everyone onto the same page and produces an agreed, timely plan, which is more effective and ethical than ignoring, unilaterally delaying, or concealing the issue.

prompts: An organization recently made substantial changes to its strategic plan. During a meeting for an ongoing project, a key stakeholder questions whether the project still aligns with the new strategy. What should the project manager do first to address this concern effectively?

A company updates its strategic direction while a project is already underway. In a project meeting, an important stakeholder asks whether the project still supports the new strategy. What should the project manager do first?
During an active project, the organization changes its strategic plan. A stakeholder challenges the project's continued alignment with that plan. What is the first effective response?
After major strategy revisions, a key stakeholder raises concern that an ongoing project may no longer fit organizational priorities. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Review the project charter and revise it to confirm strategic alignment.
correct.why: The charter links the project to business need and strategic objectives.

correct.text: Check the project charter and update it if alignment needs correction.
correct.why: Verifying alignment addresses the concern before offering assurances.

correct.text: Review and revise the charter to reflect the organization's new strategy.
correct.why: The first step is to validate whether the project still supports strategy.

distractors.text: Schedule a later meeting to discuss the stakeholder's concerns.
distractors.trap: Discussion should follow verification, not replace it.

distractors.text: Remind the stakeholder that the project began before the strategy changed.
distractors.trap: Project timing does not prove current alignment.

distractors.text: Assure the stakeholder that the project still aligns.
distractors.trap: Assurance without review lacks transparency.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to defend the original business case.
distractors.trap: Defending the past does not assess current fit.

distractors.text: Update the communications plan to include strategy updates.
distractors.trap: Communication changes do not verify alignment.

distractors.text: Pause project work until executives resolve the concern.
distractors.trap: Prematurely halts work before reviewing the charter.

explanation: The project manager should review and, if needed, revise the charter to confirm alignment with the new strategy. If organizational goals shift, the project's direction may need to pivot accordingly.

prompts: A key team member publicly claims that another team member has been speaking negatively about them and threatens to quit unless the project manager acts immediately on the conflict. Which action should come first?

During a team discussion, an important contributor says another member has been criticizing them and threatens to leave unless the project manager responds right away. What should the project manager do first?
A senior specialist accuses a colleague in front of the team of making negative comments and demands immediate action. They say they may resign if nothing is done. What is the first step?
A project team conflict becomes visible when one member publicly accuses another of bad-mouthing them and threatens to quit. Which action should the project manager take first?

correct.text: Meet with the accused team member to understand their point of view.
correct.why: The project manager needs both perspectives before resolving the conflict.

correct.text: Speak with the accused member to hear their side of the situation.
correct.why: Conflict resolution should start with understanding facts and perspectives.

correct.text: Talk privately with the accused team member to gather their perspective.
correct.why: Hearing the other side prevents acting on a one-sided account.

distractors.text: Call a full team meeting because the project is at risk.
distractors.trap: Escalates a two-person conflict unnecessarily.

distractors.text: Schedule a later meeting so emotions can cool down.
distractors.trap: Delays action despite an active conflict.

distractors.text: Remove one of the two members from the project team.
distractors.trap: Disruptive before investigating the conflict.

distractors.text: Ask the accusing member to document the complaint and wait.
distractors.trap: Adds paperwork but does not address the conflict.

distractors.text: Tell both members to stop discussing the issue at work.
distractors.trap: Suppresses conflict instead of resolving it.

distractors.text: Escalate the matter immediately to human resources.
distractors.trap: Premature before understanding both viewpoints.

explanation: Before attempting resolution, the project manager should meet with the accused team member to hear their perspective and understand the full situation.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: An agile project is in its fifth of ten iterations. After the latest retrospective, the team realizes it had to work extra hours to deliver the committed work. Which response is best?
During iteration five of a ten-iteration agile effort, a software team notices after its retrospective that overtime was needed to finish the planned work. What should the project manager do next?
A product team is halfway through a ten-sprint delivery cycle. In the retrospective, members recognize that they worked late to meet the iteration commitment. What is the best response?
On an agile initiative in its fifth iteration out of ten, the delivery team reports that completing the committed backlog required extra hours. How should this be addressed?

correct.text: Conduct a focused retrospective to help the team discover the root cause.
correct.why: Finding why overtime occurred lets the team address the real problem and protect velocity.

correct.text: Facilitate a targeted retrospective to identify what caused the overtime.
correct.why: The team needs the underlying cause before choosing a useful improvement.

correct.text: Use the retrospective to examine why the team needed extra hours.
correct.why: Root-cause learning supports sustainable pace and better future planning.

distractors.text: Ask the team to commit to less work in the next iteration.
distractors.trap: Lowering commitment sidesteps the cause and reduces delivered value.

distractors.text: Request additional delivery specialists to maintain the velocity.
distractors.trap: Adding people is not the first response to a process issue.

distractors.text: Run a mid-iteration checkpoint to verify the team is on track.
distractors.trap: Checkpoints monitor progress but do not explain the overtime.

distractors.text: Tell the team to keep working extra hours until the release ends.
distractors.trap: Normalizes an unsustainable pace.

distractors.text: Ask the product owner to remove lower-value items from the product backlog.
distractors.trap: Backlog reprioritization does not diagnose the iteration problem.

distractors.text: Escalate the missed planning accuracy to the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalation avoids team-level inspection and adaptation.

explanation: A focused retrospective helps the team identify the root cause of the overtime so it can maintain or improve velocity and remove impediments, rather than masking the symptom.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: On an agile project with a cross-functional team, the project manager learns that some delivery specialists routinely raise additional requirements that fall outside the approved scope. What should the project manager do to address this recurring behavior?
A cross-functional agile team repeatedly introduces extra requirements that are not part of the authorized scope. How should the project manager respond to prevent the pattern from continuing?
In an agile delivery group, several specialists keep proposing work beyond the agreed scope. The behavior has become routine. What action is most appropriate?
A project manager notices that members of an agile team often add requirement ideas outside the approved scope baseline. What should be done to address the recurring issue?

correct.text: Coach the team to manage scope using the appropriate framework.
correct.why: Teaching the team to manage scope builds capability to prevent future scope creep.

correct.text: Train the team on handling scope within the project framework.
correct.why: Coaching creates durable scope discipline instead of correcting one incident.

correct.text: Guide the team in applying the framework for scope control.
correct.why: The recurring behavior requires capability building, not a one-time warning.

distractors.text: Escalate to the sponsor and request replacement delivery specialists.
distractors.trap: Replacement is excessive unless project success is seriously threatened.

distractors.text: Conduct daily sync meetings with the team and stakeholders.
distractors.trap: PM-run syncs undercut self-organization and miss the behavior.

distractors.text: Explain that requirements cannot be changed without approval.
distractors.trap: A reminder alone does not build lasting scope discipline.

distractors.text: Reject every new requirement idea raised by delivery specialists.
distractors.trap: Blanket rejection blocks value discovery and learning.

distractors.text: Ask the product owner to remove these specialists from backlog refinement.
distractors.trap: Exclusion avoids coaching and weakens collaboration.

distractors.text: Document the behavior as a risk and wait for it to recur.
distractors.trap: Recording the risk does not correct the team behavior.

explanation: Coaching the team to manage scope using the appropriate agile framework equips them to avoid scope creep themselves, which is more effective than escalation, control, or one-off reminders.

prompts: A team has full access to all project data and visuals, including the Gantt chart, prioritization matrix, and story map. One team member repeatedly asks for more granular detail on a work package than the Gantt chart shows, though the chart is confirmed complete and current and the rest of the team finds it sufficiently detailed. What should the project manager do?

On a delivery project, all team members can access the project repository, roadmap visuals, and current schedule. One analyst keeps asking for work-package detail beyond what appears on the complete Gantt chart, while others are satisfied. What is the best response?

A team member frequently requests deeper detail about a work package even though the approved schedule and related project visuals are up to date and available to everyone. The rest of the team has enough information. What should the project manager do?

The project team has access to the Gantt chart, story map, and prioritization matrix. One developer still asks for more granular work-package information than the schedule view provides. How should the project manager handle this?

correct.text: Ensure the team member understands how to retrieve the granular information themselves.
correct.why: Empowering the member to self-serve closes the expectation gap without extra reporting.

correct.text: Show the team member how to access the detailed information directly.
correct.why: The information is available, so the issue is retrieval and understanding.

correct.text: Help the team member learn where to find the needed detail.
correct.why: Enabling self-service meets the need while preserving existing communication channels.

distractors.text: Create a new communication channel that provides granular updates.
distractors.trap: A dedicated channel for one person wastes resources.

distractors.text: Remind the team member that the Gantt chart is the agreed reporting format.
distractors.trap: Reminding does not resolve the unmet information need.

distractors.text: Continue to ignore the team member's requests for more detail.
distractors.trap: Ignoring the request can worsen conflict.

distractors.text: Ask the whole team to submit daily work-package detail reports.
distractors.trap: Adds overhead for a need one person can self-serve.

distractors.text: Escalate the repeated requests to the team member's functional manager.
distractors.trap: Escalation is premature for an access understanding issue.

distractors.text: Replace the Gantt chart with a more detailed schedule for everyone.
distractors.trap: The current chart is complete and adequate for the team.

explanation: The team member should be empowered to retrieve the level of detail they are authorized to access. Showing them how to self-serve granular data resolves the gap without wasting resources or escalating conflict.

prompts: A project follows a predictive life cycle, but a major deliverable is handed over incrementally to the customer. The assigned resources are experienced and reliable and are willing to make the decisions the project manager previously made after each incremental delivery on other projects. Which response is best?

In a mostly predictive project, a key deliverable is accepted by the customer in increments. The experienced team is ready to make the routine post-delivery decisions the project manager handled on past efforts. What should the project manager do?

A hybrid delivery effort uses a predictive plan while releasing one major deliverable to the customer in stages. The assigned specialists are capable and want to own decisions after each handoff. Which response is most appropriate?

A reliable, experienced team on a predictive project is delivering a large component incrementally. They are willing to take over decisions the project manager usually made after each delivery. What is the best action?

correct.text: Support the team's decisions and transfer decision-making responsibility to them.
correct.why: A servant leader empowers a capable team to own decisions in a hybrid environment.

correct.text: Empower the team to make those decisions and support their ownership.
correct.why: Capable teams should be trusted with appropriate decisions in hybrid delivery.

correct.text: Delegate the decision authority to the team and back their decisions.
correct.why: Transferring authority supports ownership when the team has the needed capability.

distractors.text: Tell the team that the project manager must still make the decisions.
distractors.trap: Retaining all decisions contradicts empowering a capable team.

distractors.text: Check with the value representative before letting the resources decide.
distractors.trap: Separate approval is unnecessary for routine team authority.

distractors.text: Schedule an executive oversight panel to approve the decision shift.
distractors.trap: Oversight panels do not approve routine team decision-making.

distractors.text: Require the team to submit each decision for project manager approval.
distractors.trap: Approval gates keep ownership with the project manager.

distractors.text: Delay decision changes until the project fully adopts agile methods.
distractors.trap: Empowerment can be used in hybrid and predictive contexts.

distractors.text: Ask the customer to decide after each incremental handover.
distractors.trap: Customer acceptance does not replace team delivery decisions.

explanation: In a hybrid environment a capable team can own decisions and outcomes. The servant-leader project manager should empower them by supporting and transferring decision-making responsibility.

prompts: A CTO is enthusiastic about the organization's recent adoption of servant leadership and asks all project managers to apply it, but seems unsure of how it works in practice. Which statement best captures how the project manager should respond?
An executive wants project managers to use servant leadership across the portfolio but asks whether it only applies in certain delivery methods. What should a project manager explain?
A technology leader has endorsed servant leadership and wants guidance on when it is appropriate. Which response best reflects its use in project environments?
After adopting servant leadership, a senior leader asks how broadly project managers can apply the approach. What is the best statement?

correct.text: Explain that servant leadership can be adapted and applied to any project situation.
correct.why: Servant leadership principles are not limited by method, client focus, or budget.

correct.text: State that servant leadership principles can be tailored for any project context.
correct.why: The approach is broadly applicable when adapted to the project environment.

correct.text: Clarify that servant leadership is useful across project types when tailored appropriately.
correct.why: Its focus on enabling people applies beyond a single life cycle.

distractors.text: Note that servant leadership is better suited to projects using agile methods.
distractors.trap: Servant leadership is not exclusive to agile projects.

distractors.text: Clarify that servant leadership practices center on the client's preferences.
distractors.trap: It serves the team and stakeholders, not just the client.

distractors.text: State that servant leadership works only for projects with substantial resources.
distractors.trap: It applies to projects of any size or budget.

distractors.text: Explain that servant leadership replaces governance on adaptive projects.
distractors.trap: Leadership style does not remove governance needs.

distractors.text: Limit servant leadership to teams that are already high performing.
distractors.trap: It can help teams develop performance.

distractors.text: Use servant leadership only when stakeholders request a collaborative style.
distractors.trap: Its applicability is not dependent on stakeholder preference.

explanation: Servant leadership focuses on understanding and supporting team members to enable high performance. While some approaches rely on it more, its principles can be applied to any project situation regardless of method or budget.

prompts: A company is adopting an agile approach to meet customer needs. Previously it used specialized resources and project managers assigned to projects for fixed periods. What should the organization change to better support agile resource planning?

An organization moving to agile has historically staffed projects with narrow specialists and temporary project manager assignments. Which resource-planning change would best support the new approach?
A firm wants to improve customer responsiveness by adopting agile methods. Its old model relied on specialists assigned only for set windows. What should it change?
A department is shifting from fixed-period specialist assignments to agile delivery. How should it structure resources to better fit agile planning?

correct.text: The team should consist of generalists and specialists able to adapt to changing needs.
correct.why: A stable, cross-functional mix supports agile adaptability and collaboration.

correct.text: Build stable teams with both broad and specialized skills for changing needs.
correct.why: Agile teams benefit from cross-functional capability and flexible collaboration.

correct.text: Use a cross-functional team combining specialists with adaptable generalists.
correct.why: This structure helps the team respond as priorities and work evolve.

distractors.text: The value representative should request specialists only when they are needed.
distractors.trap: On-demand specialists disrupt flow and team stability.

distractors.text: The agile team should decide which customer requirements get delivered.
distractors.trap: Prioritization belongs to the value representative.

distractors.text: Customer involvement should be reduced to avoid disruption and confusion.
distractors.trap: Agile depends on ongoing customer involvement.

distractors.text: Project managers should rotate team members after each iteration.
distractors.trap: Frequent rotation damages continuity and learning.

distractors.text: Assign only deep technical specialists to maximize individual efficiency.
distractors.trap: Narrow specialization limits adaptability and collaboration.

distractors.text: Centralize all resource decisions in the functional departments.
distractors.trap: Functional control can slow agile responsiveness.

explanation: An agile team works together and continuously improves, so a stable, agreed mix of generalists and specialists for the project supports cross-functionality and adaptability to evolving needs.

eco_task: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
topic: Support Team Performance by Providing Adequate Training
prompts: A team member unfamiliar with the subject matter is struggling to complete a complex deliverable. What should the agile project manager do to ensure the team member can handle a similar challenge in the future?
On an agile team, a developer lacks domain knowledge and is having difficulty finishing a complex item. What should the project manager do so the person can succeed on similar work later?
A specialist new to the business area is struggling with a complicated deliverable. The project manager wants the member to be ready for this type of work in the future. What is best?
An agile team member does not understand the subject area well enough to complete a difficult deliverable comfortably. How should the project manager support future capability?

correct.text: Ensure the team member receives adequate training and becomes comfortable with the subject matter.
correct.why: Training closes the knowledge gap so the member can grow and succeed later.

correct.text: Provide training so the team member can build comfort with the subject.
correct.why: Capability improves when the specific knowledge gap is addressed directly.

correct.text: Arrange appropriate learning support for the unfamiliar subject matter.
correct.why: Skill development prepares the member for similar future challenges.

distractors.text: Have the member take on more stories in this subject until comfortable.
distractors.trap: More work without training can hurt quality and velocity.

distractors.text: Have the member avoid stories in this subject until comfortable.
distractors.trap: Avoidance never closes the knowledge gap.

distractors.text: Replace the member with a more experienced person to improve velocity.
distractors.trap: Replacement denies growth and can harm team dynamics.

distractors.text: Ask another specialist to finish the deliverable and move on.
distractors.trap: Solves the task but not the future capability.

distractors.text: Escalate the performance concern to the functional manager.
distractors.trap: Escalation is premature before support and training.

distractors.text: Reduce the acceptance criteria for this deliverable.
distractors.trap: Lowering quality does not develop the member.
